Title: The Plant Polyester Cutin: Biosynthesis, Structure, and Biological Roles

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports cutin deposition by exporting cutin monomers across the plasma membrane
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hydrophobic boundary formation, structural continuity of cuticle, water barrier support, pathogen barrier, organ separation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABCG11 is implicated in cutin deposition/export. 2. It functions at the plasma membrane in monoacylglycerol export. 3. Loss of function alters cuticle formation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review article; transporter role summarized from prior studies, not newly tested here.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering target for improving cuticle integrity and associated stress tolerance, but broad cuticle effects may cause pleiotropy.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered cuticle permeability and organ fusion/cuticle defects if misregulated.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports cutin deposition by exporting cutin monomers across the plasma membrane
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hydrophobic boundary formation, water barrier support, pathogen barrier, organ separation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABCG13 is implicated in cutin deposition/export. 2. It contributes to plasma membrane export of monoacylglycerol monomers. 3. Transport is required for proper cuticle assembly.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review article; role summarized from referenced studies.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for modifying cuticle assembly and stress resilience.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May broadly affect epidermal barrier traits.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports cutin deposition by exporting cutin monomers across the plasma membrane
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hydrophobic boundary formation, water barrier support, pathogen barrier, organ separation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABCG32 is implicated in cutin deposition/export. 2. It participates in moving monomers out of epidermal cells. 3. Proper export is necessary for cuticle formation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review article; role summarized from referenced studies.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can alter pathogen interactions and cuticle permeability when defective.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potential transporter of lipids through the cell wall, but specific role in cutin transport is not demonstrated
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced wax-transport studies; no direct cutin validation stated
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LTPG1 is proposed as a possible lipid transporter in the apoplast. 2. A specific role in cutin transport has not yet been demonstrated. 3. Evidence is therefore speculative for cutin metabolism.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in Table 1 transport section, but text explicitly states specific role in cutin transport has yet to be demonstrated.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potential transporter of lipids through the cell wall, but specific role in cutin transport is not demonstrated
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced wax-transport studies; no direct cutin validation stated
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LTPG2 is proposed as a possible apoplastic lipid transporter. 2. Direct evidence for cutin transport is lacking. 3. Its involvement in cutin remains speculative.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in Table 1 transport section, but cutin-specific function is unproven.

Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Outermost portion of epidermal cell wall
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for proper cuticle organization; mutant shows disorganized cuticle intermingled with cell wall and increased cutin polymer amount rather than reduced polymerization
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cuticle structural integrity, water barrier support, pathogen barrier, organ separation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; localization study; cuticle composition/structure analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BDG is required for normal cuticle organization. 2. bdg mutants have highly disorganized cuticle mixed with cell wall. 3. Increased cutin polymer in bdg argues against BDG being the main polymerase.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function remains unknown; likely not a direct cutin polymerase.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for modifying cuticle architecture, though mechanism is unclear.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cuticle disorganization and organ fusion when defective.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal accumulation of dihydroxy 16C fatty acids in cutin, but precise role in cutin biosynthesis remains unclear
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cuticle integrity, organ separation, barrier properties
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; in vitro enzyme assay
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. dcr mutants show near-complete loss of dihydroxy 16C fatty acids in cutin. 2. DCR has diacylglycerol acyltransferase activity in vitro. 3. Cytosolic localization makes a direct role in cutin polymerization unlikely.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function categorized as unknown in Table 1; implicated in cutin formation but mechanistically unresolved.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for altering cutin monomer profile, but unclear mechanism limits application.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Defects can disrupt cuticle structure and organ separation.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies activated precursor pool for cutin monomer synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Attaches coenzyme A to free fatty acids to generate acyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes cutin and wax production; semi-redundant with LACS2
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hydrophobic cuticle formation, water barrier support, pathogen barrier, organ separation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LACS1 activates free fatty acids for cutin monomer synthesis. 2. LACS1 and LACS2 function semi-redundantly in cutin and wax production. 3. Yeast expression supports a role in fatty acid uptake/ER import.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review indicates activation likely precedes oxidation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be targeted to alter cuticle load and barrier properties.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cuticle defects and altered pathogen susceptibility if reduced.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies activated precursor pool for cutin monomer synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Attaches coenzyme A to free fatty acids to generate acyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes cutin and wax production; lacs2 mutants are cutin-defective and show altered pathogen interactions
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hydrophobic cuticle formation, pathogen barrier, organ separation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; cutin profiling; pathogen assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LACS2 is a core upstream enzyme in cutin monomer biosynthesis. 2. It acts semi-redundantly with LACS1 in cutin/wax production. 3. lacs2 mutants have defective cuticles and altered pathogen resistance/susceptibility profiles.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Referenced as a cutin biosynthetic mutant with higher Botrytis resistance but increased susceptibility to Pseudomonas syringae.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulation may tune pathogen barrier traits, but outcomes are pathosystem dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher cuticle permeability; contrasting pathogen responses; possible organ fusion/cuticle defects.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies activated precursor pool for cutin monomer synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Attaches coenzyme A to free fatty acids to generate acyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potential contributor to cuticle formation, but not experimentally validated for cutin in this review
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LACS3 transcripts are enriched in epidermal cells. 2. It may contribute to cuticle formation. 3. Direct experimental validation for cutin biosynthesis is still lacking.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms direct oxygenated monomer precursors for cutin polymer
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Acyl-CoA-linked long-chain fatty acids or long-chain fatty acids
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the terminal ω-carbon of fatty acids and may contribute to further oxidation leading to diacids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal cutin composition and cuticle structure; mutants are deficient in diacids and have altered pathogen susceptibility
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Barrier integrity, pathogen defense, organ separation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; heterologous expression enzyme assay; cutin profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP86A2 is a cutin ω-hydroxylase. 2. cyp86a2 mutants have altered cutin composition and abnormal cuticle structures. 3. CYP86A2 may also contribute to diacid synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes enzymes catalyzing full diacid synthesis remain unconfirmed, but CYP86A2 is implicated.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate to engineer cuticle monomer composition.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Defects increase susceptibility to some pathogens and disrupt cuticle structure.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms direct oxygenated monomer precursors for cutin polymer
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the terminal ω-carbon of fatty acids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; heterologous expression enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP86A4 is implicated in cutin ω-hydroxylation. 2. Mutants have altered cutin composition. 3. It belongs to the CYP86A family that catalyzes terminal hydroxylation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific structural defects are emphasized more strongly for cyp86a2 and cyp86a8 than CYP86A4.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to alter monomer oxygenation patterns.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Potentially forms oxygenated monomer precursors for cutin polymer
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Putatively hydroxylates the terminal ω-carbon of fatty acids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP86A7 is a putative cutin-related ω-hydroxylase. 2. Its sequence similarity and SHN1 inducibility suggest cutin involvement. 3. Direct biochemical or genetic validation is lacking.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Speculative function in review and Table 1.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms direct oxygenated monomer precursors for cutin polymer
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the terminal ω-carbon of fatty acids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal cutin composition and cuticle structure
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; heterologous expression enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP86A8 is a cutin ω-hydroxylase. 2. cyp86a8 mutants have altered cutin composition and abnormal cuticle structures. 3. It contributes to synthesis of oxygenated cutin monomers.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms major dihydroxy cutin monomer precursor
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds a midchain hydroxyl to an ω-hydroxy C16 fatty acid
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for dihydroxy cutin monomer accumulation in petals
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall, especially petals
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; heterologous expression enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP77A6 is the identified enzyme for cutin midchain hydroxylation. 2. cyp77a6 mutants lose the dihydroxy cutin monomer in petals. 3. Enzyme assays indicate ω-hydroxylation precedes midchain hydroxylation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Provides ordering evidence for oxidation sequence in cutin monomer synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering specific monomer branching capacity in cutin.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds epoxide groups to unsaturated fatty acids in vitro
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Possible contributor to epoxidized cutin monomer formation, but in planta role is not known
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP77A4 can epoxidize unsaturated fatty acids in vitro. 2. Whether it performs this step in cutin synthesis remains unknown. 3. Its cutin role is therefore tentative.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function in Table 1 shown as epoxidase; text states in planta relevance unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms cutin monomer building blocks
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers oxidized acyl group from acyl-CoA to the sn-2 position of glycerol-3-phosphate and removes phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required redundantly with GPAT8 for leaf and stem cutin formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPAT4 synthesizes 2-monoacylglycerol cutin monomers. 2. It acts redundantly with GPAT8 in leaf and stem cutin formation. 3. It prefers oxidized fatty acids and the sn-2 position.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Possible functional association with CYP86-type P450s
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to increase or modify cutin monomer supply.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms cutin monomer building blocks
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers oxidized acyl group from acyl-CoA to glycerol-3-phosphate at sn-2 and removes phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPAT6 generates 2-monoacylglycerol monomers for cutin. 2. It contributes specifically to petal cutin synthesis. 3. The enzyme prefers oxidized fatty acids and the sn-2 position.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Possible functional association with CYP86-type P450s
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Ectopic GPAT5 plus CYP86A1 increased cutin, whereas single genes did not; suggests pathway compatibility constraints among GPAT/P450 modules.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be leveraged to alter organ-specific cutin deposition.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May affect floral epidermis development if misexpressed.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms cutin monomer building blocks
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers oxidized acyl group from acyl-CoA to glycerol-3-phosphate at sn-2 and removes phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required redundantly with GPAT4 for leaf and stem cutin formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPAT8 synthesizes 2-monoacylglycerol cutin monomers. 2. It acts redundantly with GPAT4 in leaf and stem cutin formation. 3. It has phosphatase activity and prefers oxidized fatty acyl substrates.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Possible functional association with CYP86-type P450s
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to boost cutin monomer production.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Provides modified monomer for incorporation into cutin polymer
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers ferulic acid from CoA to the terminal hydroxyl of an ω-hydroxy fatty acid
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls incorporation of ferulate into cutin-related monomers
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Polyester lipid biopolymer with phenylpropanoid component
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: May influence cuticle architecture and properties
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; mutant analysis summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DCF catalyzes ferulate transfer onto ω-hydroxy fatty acids. 2. It explains how ferulate is attached to cutin-associated aliphatic monomers. 3. Export and subsequent polymer incorporation of alkyl ferulates remain unknown.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This addresses phenolic monomer incorporation rather than bulk aliphatic polymerization.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modify phenolic reinforcement of cuticle.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown effects on cuticle mechanics and permeability.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms cutin polymer from monomeric 2-monoacylglycerols
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers hydroxyacyl groups from one 2-monoacylglycerol molecule to a growing cutin oligomer/polymer chain, releasing glycerol
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functions as a cutin synthase/polyester synthase
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural scaffold for wax deposition, barrier integrity, viscoelastic cuticle matrix
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro polymerization assay with recombinant enzyme; phylogenetic analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CUS1 is an ancient and conserved cutin synthase family member. 2. It polymerizes 2-monoacylglycerol substrates into cutin in vitro with negligible hydrolytic activity. 3. Different CUS proteins vary in substrate efficiency, suggesting substrate specialization.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In this review Arabidopsis CUS1 is summarized, but strongest in vivo evidence for fruit cutin polymerization is from tomato SlCUS1/CD1.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong engineering candidate to increase polymerization capacity of cutin.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misregulation could alter cuticle architecture and permeability.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms cutin polymer from monomeric 2-monoacylglycerols
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers hydroxyacyl groups from 2-MHG to the terminus of a growing cutin polymer chain, releasing glycerol; may favor midchain esterification in vivo
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for tomato fruit cutin polymer accumulation; null mutants reduce polymeric cutin by >95% and accumulate 2-MHG
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural scaffold, water barrier support, pathogen barrier, fruit surface integrity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Null mutant analysis; RNAi suppression; recombinant enzyme assay; hydroxyl-group labeling before depolymerization; cutin quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SlCUS1/CD1 is a core cutin polymerase in tomato fruit. 2. cd1 mutants show >95% loss of polymeric cutin and accumulation of 2-MHG. 3. Recombinant enzyme polymerizes 2-MHG in vitro, while in vivo evidence suggests preference for midchain esterification.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: RNAi suppression lines; promoter details not provided
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Residual cutin in cd1 suggests additional CUS paralogs or alternative polymerization mechanisms.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent target for modifying fruit cuticle load, permeability, shelf life, and pathogen resistance.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe loss causes thin cuticle, nanopores, elevated transpiration, and higher Botrytis susceptibility.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Contributes to formation of diacid cutin monomers
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Proposed to participate in oxidation of ω-hydroxylated fatty acids to diacids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal diacid content in cutin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cuticle on aerial epidermal cell outer wall
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HOTHEAD mutants are specifically deficient in cutin diacids. 2. The gene is implicated in diacid synthesis. 3. Exact enzymatic mechanism remains unconfirmed.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review states enzymes catalyzing these oxidation steps remain unconfirmed; HOTHEAD is implicated genetically.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to alter cross-linking-capable monomer abundance.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could affect cuticle architecture and barrier traits.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cutin biosynthesis and broader cuticle formation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Cutin biosynthesis genes; SHN1-regulated promoters in Arabidopsis and rice; CYP86A7 is induced by SHN1
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of floral cutin and epidermal cell morphology; direct regulator of cutin biosynthesis genes
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cuticle barrier integrity, water loss limitation, organ separation, pathogen barrier
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant/overexpression evidence from cited studies; promoter binding assays
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Direct positive regulator of cutin biosynthesis genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SHN1 is a positive regulator of cutin formation. 2. It binds promoters of cutin biosynthesis genes in Arabidopsis and rice. 3. SHN1 is therefore considered a direct regulator of cutin biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Associated with floral cutin and epidermal development
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Associated with floral cutin and epidermal development
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Developmental programs; in tomato, TAGL1 regulates SHN1/SHN3
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad epidermal developmental effects if misexpressed.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising master regulator for engineering cuticle traits, but likely pleiotropic due to developmental roles.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of three largely redundant SHN genes.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of floral cutin and epidermal cell morphology
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Redundant positive regulator of cutin formation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SHN2 is one of the redundant AP2 regulators of floral cutin. 2. It positively regulates cuticle-associated traits. 3. It functions with SHN1 and SHN3.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulator for cuticle engineering, likely best used in combination with tissue-specific control.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct target-gene evidence is stronger for SHN1 than for SHN2.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of floral cutin and epidermal cell morphology
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Redundant positive regulator of cutin formation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SHN3 is one of the redundant AP2 regulators of floral cutin. 2. It positively regulates cuticle-associated traits. 3. In tomato, TAGL1 regulates SHN3 homologous pathway context.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Developmental programs; TAGL1 in tomato regulates SHN3
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of three largely redundant SHN genes.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Suppresses HDG1 activity/effect on cuticle development
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein interaction evidence and genetic studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Suppressor of cuticle development through HDG1 interaction
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CFL1 is a negative regulator of cuticle development. 2. It interacts with HDG1. 3. This interaction modulates epidermal cuticle formation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering CFL1 may broadly affect epidermal development.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible derepression target to enhance cuticle deposition, with caution for pleiotropy.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cuticle synthesis and epidermal cell identity
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positive regulation of cuticle synthesis program
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of tomato fruit cuticle synthesis; cd2 mutants are cutin deficient
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Water barrier support, pathogen barrier, fruit surface integrity
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major positive regulator of fruit cuticle deposition
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CD2 is an HD-ZIP IV positive regulator of tomato fruit cuticle synthesis. 2. cd2 fruit has only about 2% of wild-type cutin. 3. Reduced cutin in cd2 correlates with altered transpiration and pathogen susceptibility contexts discussed in the review.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Fruit epidermis inferred from phenotype context
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Fruit epidermis inferred from phenotype context
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe cutin reduction compromises cuticle function.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important regulator for fruit cuticle engineering, but strong perturbation may severely weaken barrier function.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because review treats tomato cutin-deficient cd2 phenotype as core evidence.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positive regulation of cuticle synthesis program
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Part of HD-ZIP IV epidermal regulatory module controlling cuticle
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANL2 is an HD-ZIP IV positive regulator of cuticle synthesis. 2. It links epidermal identity with cutin production. 3. Its effects are part of broader epidermal developmental control.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential cuticle regulator, likely requiring spatially restricted manipulation.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation modulated by protein interaction
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positive regulation of cuticle synthesis program
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of cuticle development; activity is modulated by CFL1
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic studies; interaction evidence with CFL1
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive regulator within epidermal identity network
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HDG1 positively regulates cuticle development. 2. Its activity is affected by interaction with CFL1. 3. This places HDG1 in a regulatory module balancing cuticle deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate regulator for cuticle improvement if controlled precisely.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Negative regulation of cuticle-associated stress-responsive transcriptional network
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses SHN1/2/3 and BDG expression, directly or indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Negative regulator whose mutation increases expression of cuticle-associated genes
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Barrier integrity and stress-associated cuticle responses
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant expression analysis summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-linked repressor connecting abiotic stress response with cuticle gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NFXL2 functions as an apparent repressor of abiotic stress responses. 2. nfxl2 mutation increases expression of SHN1, SHN2, SHN3, and BDG. 3. It therefore negatively regulates a cuticle-associated transcriptional network.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may broadly alter stress responses.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful repressor target to elevate cuticle-related pathways under stress, but broad stress-network effects are expected.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper notes few environmental response mediators are identified.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of cuticle and epidermis formation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive regulator in epidermal cuticle network
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB16 positively regulates cuticle and epidermis formation. 2. It functions with MYB106. 3. Their effects include cuticle-associated structural and compositional phenotypes.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct cutin target-gene evidence not definite.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of cuticle and epidermis formation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Upstream activator linking MYB and SHN modules in cuticle development
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB106 controls multiple aspects of cuticle and epidermis formation. 2. SHN1 is regulated by MYB106. 3. MYB106 therefore acts upstream in the cutin regulatory network.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential upstream target for boosting cuticle deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct cutin target-gene specificity remains uncertain for many TFs except SHN1.
